Metadata.Coram: Wong Sung Boon v Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and another
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Wong Sung Boon

Facts.Text: From 2011-04-01, Wong Sung Boon was Senior Managing Director and Chief Executive Officer of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.
Wong's appointment as Senior Managing Director was governed by a Letter of Appointment dated 2016-04-01, described as his Employment Contract.
The Employment Contract for Wong as Senior Managing Director was renewed with an employment period from 2017-04-01 to 2018-03-31.

A media release in 2014-04 stated that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would offer one-stop financial solutions for businesses, including hire purchase or leasing, sales and leaseback schemes, credit control outsourcing services, litigation services and other financial solutions.

Around 2014-04, public materials described Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and its Innovation Office in Singapore as operating a consultancy business, building analytics, and expanding into wearable technology, telepresence, artificial intelligence and a cloud-based audio tour guide service.

Around 2001 to 2002, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd began offering project management services involving planning projects, sourcing and liaising with suppliers, structuring contracts, coordinating with suppliers and customers, and monitoring milestones.

Under his terms of appointment as Managing Director and later Senior Managing Director, Wong was required to devote his best efforts to promote, develop and extend the business of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd in Singapore.
In September 2017, Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd was commissioned by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to conduct an audit investigation into certain past transactions.
On 2017-10-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd suspended Wong from his role with full pay pending the outcome of the audit investigation.

On 2017-11-08, Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd issued a report of its audit investigation concerning past transactions of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd involving I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd and Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd.

On 2017-12-07, Fujifilm's Global Audit Division produced a special audit report highlighting irregularities in Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's transactions with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd.
On 2017-10-30, Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd held a teleconference with Wong as part of its audit investigation.
On 2017-11-30, Wong met Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd in connection with the audit investigation.

On 2011-10-11, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd entered into a Master Services Agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to supply, construct, install and commission base transceiver station equipment and facilities for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for approximately SGD 4.3 million, referred to as the IC1 Contract.

On 2012-03-16, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd entered into a Master Services Agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to supply, construct and install fibre optic infrastructure for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for approximately SGD 5.59 million, referred to as the IC2 Contract.
On 2011-11-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's Legal Department signed the IC1 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd.
On 2012-04-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd executed the IC2 Contract after the credit evaluation was completed.
On 2011-10-20, a director of I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd, Raden, signed the IC1 Contract.

In early 2012, during a Business Review Meeting, Wong informed Katsuhiko Yanagawa, then President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had entered into the IC1 Contract and intended to enter into the IC2 Contract.
Subsequently, I-Connect defaulted on some payments under the IC2 Contract of $557,921.50, referred to as the IC Debt.
In June 2014, Wong approved the write-off of the IC Debt.
At the annual general meeting to approve FXS' 2014 audited accounts, the accounts reflected the IC Debt as written off.

On 2014-03-05, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a sales agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to supply offshore diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for $1 million, referred to as the MOS1 Contract.

On 2014-12-01, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a sales agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to sell diving equipment and repair, refurbish and test a transformer and capacitor load bank for $2 million, referred to as the MOS2 Contract.

On 2015-08-28, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a rental agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to lease diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for $1 million, referred to as the MOS3 Contract.

On 2015-11-04, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd entered into a rental agreement for Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to lease diving equipment and provide repair and reconditioning work on a transformer and capacitor load bank unit for $1,007,496, referred to as the MOS4 Contract.
In early 2014, during a Business Review Meeting, Wong informed Honda, then APO President, that FXS was considering transacting with MOS on a project relating to diving equipment.

On 2014-12-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a purchase order to Serve Tech Container Specialist Pte Ltd for fabrication and installation works of diving equipment related to the MOS2 Contract, subject to standard terms and conditions.

On 2013-12-06, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a sale agreement with Supreme Lion Holding Pte Ltd to supply products and services for installation, testing and commissioning of power control units for woodwork machinery systems.

On 2013-12-19, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a purchase order to Hyper Marketing (Singapore) Pte Ltd for woodwork machinery system products and services related to the Supreme Lion transaction, subject to standard purchase order terms and conditions.

FXS adopted the Abacus System, which had limitations in that the description for a transaction had to be chosen from a drop-down box and there was no description corresponding to an instalment sale agreement.
On 2011-10-19, the CE Form for the IC1 Contract was submitted.
On 2012-03-29, Wong reviewed the CE Pack for the IC2 Contract.

FXS' credit evaluation process involved credit evaluation forms, scorecards and reports, and could use internal records and publicly available information when listed supporting documents were not available.
FXS' 2015 Operating Procedure Manual was silent on the requirement for personal guarantees.

FXS had a 2006 Communication Matrix that required concurrence from the President of Asia Pacific Operations for certain categories of business activities, including business activities outside the territory and write-off of bad debts above specified thresholds.
On 2013-12-02, an email was sent to various persons including Wong attaching the 2013 Communication Matrix.

The CE Report for the MOS2 Contract reflected FXS as financing the construction material for diving equipment, with the financed amount at $1 million, an interest rate of 3.5% and monthly payments of $86,250 over 12 months.
FXS prepared a manual costing sheet for the MOS2 Contract showing an internal cost of $1,930,000 and total revenue of $2,000,000, reflecting a gross profit of $70,000.
FXS prepared a manual costing sheet for the MOS3 Contract showing an internal cost of $965,000 and total contract revenue of $1,000,000.
FXS prepared a manual costing sheet for the MOS4 Contract showing an internal cost of $955,480 and total contract revenue of $1,007,496.
For the MOS2 Contract, FXS obtained personal guarantees from three individuals associated with MOS: Samuel Yeo, Rachael Yeo and Elizabeth Lee.
For the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, FXS obtained personal guarantees from four individuals associated with MOS: Samuel Yeo, Rachael Yeo, Elizabeth Lee and Adrian Lim.
A search conducted after letters of demand were sent regarding the IC Debt showed that a bankruptcy application had been filed against Suleman in July 2013.
A search result made known to Wong only in June 2014 showed that Raden was driving a taxi for a living.
The CE Form showed that, based on the income tax assessments, Samuel's and Rachael's combined annual income at the material time was about $675,000.
The CE Form for the MOS4 Contract showed Samuel's and Rachael's combined annual income based on the income tax assessments had increased to about $1.46 million.
MOS had paid to FXS more than 96% of the total contract price of the MOS1 Contract.
Before the MOS3 Contract was entered into, MOS had been making prompt payments to FXS under the MOS2 Contract then amounting to 70% of the contract price.
When the MOS4 Contract was executed, MOS had already fully paid up on the MOS1 Contract and had been making prompt payments under the MOS3 Contract then amounting to 40% of the contract price.
Subsequently, MOS defaulted on payments under the MOS Contracts, amounting to $2,322,464.28.
Supreme Lion had paid FXS pursuant to the SL Contract and FXS had not suffered any loss.

On 2017-12-21, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd issued a termination notice to Wong stating that his employment was terminated with immediate effect pursuant to specified clauses of his Employment Contract and that he would not receive notice pay or an end of term payment.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company's summary dismissal of its CEO without notice was contractually justified, and whether its parent company unlawfully induced the termination.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Singapore employment and company law, an employer may summarily terminate an employee's fixed-term contract only where the employee's conduct falls within contractually specified grounds such as gross default or misconduct, material damage caused intentionally or through negligence, or conduct grossly incompatible with the due and faithful discharge of duties. Directors and senior managers are exercise reasonable care, skill and diligence comparable to a reasonable person in their position, act in good faith in the interests of the company, comply with internal approval matrices and procedures, and manage credit and transactional risk through documented processes. Where a summary termination is not justified, damages are generally assessed by reference to the minimum legal obligation, limiting recovery to the period and payments the employer would have been bound to provide if it had exercised its contractual power to terminate in the least costly lawful manner, including salary in lieu of notice and other contractual payments that would have accrued within that period.
Party_Details.Application: A CEO was summarily dismissed and denied a variable bonus following an internal audit. The dispute assesses whether the termination was a valid exercise of contractual rights based on alleged breaches of duty, and whether the regional parent company committed an actionable tort by procuring this dismissal.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court analysed the structure and scope of Wong's Employment Contract, including clause 8.1 on termination by three months' basic salary in lieu of notice and clause 10 on summary dismissal for specified categories of conduct such as causing material damage, gross default or misconduct, and conduct grossly incompatible with the due and faithful discharge of duties. It examined the factual matrix of the I-Connect, MOS and Supreme Lion transactions, the Deloitte Report and the Fujifilm Global Audit Division special audit report, and the internal Communication Matrices and credit evaluation processes. For the I-Connect transactions, the court found that the IC1 and IC2 Contracts were project management-type arrangements with back-to-back supplier contracts, that the Tsalasa Contract contained warranties and undertakings protecting Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd, and that there was no evidence of I-Connect alleging breach or of suppliers failing to perform. The court accepted evidence that Wong consulted relevant departments, that the Legal Department cleared the IC1 Contract, and that the then President of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa, orally approved the IC1 and IC2 Contracts at a Business Review Meeting, thereby satisfying or curing any Communication Matrix approval requirement. The court held that although Wong did not initially obtain formal approval before entering the IC1 Contract or before writing off the IC Debt, subsequent approval by the regional president and the board, including Honda's acceptance of the audited accounts reflecting the write-off, rectified any procedural breach. It concluded that these matters did not cause material damage to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and did not amount to conduct grossly incompatible with Wong's duties or to gross default or misconduct under clause 10(b), (h) or (i).

For the MOS transactions, the court rejected the defendants' characterisation of the MOS2, MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts as pure loans disguised as sales or rentals. It relied on the contractual documents, supplier quotations and purchase orders, manual costing sheets, and testimony from finance staff to find that the MOS2 Contract was an instalment sale with a gross profit margin and that the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, though documented as rental agreements due to limitations of the Abacus billing system, were in substance asset sale or lease transactions with underlying goods and services supplied by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd through its suppliers. The court noted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd contracted directly with suppliers, that goods were delivered and no complaints were raised by MOS, and that the Legal Department had cleared the MOS2 Contract. It accepted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was already providing commercial financing and project management services and that the MOS transactions fell within an expanded business model encouraged by the regional management. The court found that Wong obtained multiple personal guarantees from individuals associated with MOS, that detailed credit evaluations were performed, and that MOS had a positive payment history before later default. It held that the defendants failed to show that Wong exposed the company to unreasonable or unnecessary liability or that he failed to exercise reasonable diligence.

Regarding the Supreme Lion transaction, the court found that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a sale agreement to supply woodwork machinery system products and services, that it contracted with Hyper Marketing (Singapore) Pte Ltd under purchase orders incorporating standard terms, warranties and indemnities, and that Supreme Lion paid the contract price without raising complaints. The court rejected the assertion that the supplier lacked capability based solely on an ACRA search conducted years later and held that there was no evidence of loss or improper exposure arising from this transaction.

The court evaluated the Communication Matrices and concluded that while certain approvals were formally required for business activities outside the territory, new business activities, and write-offs above specified thresholds, oral approvals by the Presidents of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa and later Honda, satisfied or cured these requirements. It accepted that the matrices did not convert every procedural misstep into a repudiatory breach and noted that clause 10(c) of the Employment Contract contemplated warnings and repetition for violations of work regulations. Applying the standard of reasonable care and diligence from Scintronix and the principles on serious misconduct and repudiatory breach from Phosagro, the court held that Wong's conduct did not reach the threshold of gross default or misconduct or conduct grossly incompatible with his duties, and that any technical breaches of internal procedures were either rectified or non-prejudicial.

On the counterclaim, the court dismissed Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's claim for approximately SGD 2.88 million, comprising the IC Debt and MOS outstanding amounts, because it found no actionable breach of fiduciary or contractual duties by Wong that caused those losses. On the lawfulness of the summary dismissal, the court concluded that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was not entitled to rely on clause 10(b), (h) or (i) to summarily dismiss Wong without notice or payment in lieu. It then applied the minimum legal obligation rule from Alexander Proudfoot and Gunton, as elaborated in Aldabe Fermin, to determine damages. The court reasoned that, given the audit findings and the employer's subsequent actions, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would have chosen to terminate Wong's employment on 2017-12-21 by exercising its contractual right under clause 8.1 to pay three months' basic salary in lieu of notice rather than allowing the contract to run to 2018-03-31 or giving working notice. It therefore assessed Wong's entitlement on the basis of a lawful termination with three months' salary in lieu of notice and considered his claims for notice pay, end-of-term payment, variable bonus and salary in lieu of accrued leave accordingly. Overall, the court held that the summary dismissal was unlawful and that Wong was entitled to contractual remedies, while the defendants' allegations of misconduct and their counterclaim were not made out.

Metadata.Coram: Wong Sung Boon v Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and another
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd is a company whose business initially centred on wholesale, repair and maintenance of office equipment described as print products.
Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd is owned by Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, which in turn is owned by Fuji Xerox Co Ltd, a company jointly owned by Xerox Limited and Fujifilm Holdings Corporation.

Around 2001 to 2002, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd diversified its business to include providing financing, infrastructural support, project management services and selling products manufactured by third parties and non-Fuji Xerox-related entities.

In 2014-04, a media release stated that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would offer a full suite of financial solutions including hire purchase or leasing, sales and leaseback schemes, credit control outsourcing services and litigation services.

Public materials around 2014 described Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and its Innovation Office in Singapore as operating consultancy-type services and expanding into wearable technology, telepresence, artificial intelligence and a cloud-based audio tour guide service.

From April to June 2017, an Independent Investigation Committee established by Fujifilm conducted an investigation into accounting practices in Fuji Xerox Australia and Fuji Xerox New Zealand, subsidiaries of FXAP.
In September 2017, FXS commissioned Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd to conduct an audit investigation expected to take place between 13 and 29 September 2017.
On 19 October 2017, FXS suspended Wong with full pay pending the outcome of the Audit Investigation.
On 8 November 2017, Deloitte issued a report with findings on the Audit Investigation which revealed irregularities in FXS' past transactions with I-Connect and Supreme Lion.
On 7 December 2017, Fujifilm's Global Audit Division presented a special audit report that highlighted irregularities in FXS' transactions with I-Connect, Supreme Lion and MOS.

On 11 October 2011, FXS entered into a Master Services Agreement with I-Connect for supply, construction, installation and commissioning of BTS equipment and facilities to I-Connect for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for about $4.3 million.

On 16 March 2012, FXS entered into a Master Services Agreement with I-Connect for supply, construct and install a Fiber Optic Infrastructure to I-Connect for a satellite infrastructure project in Indonesia for about $5.59 million.
On 1 November 2011, FXS' Legal Department signed and dated the IC1 Contract.
The parties signed the IC2 Contract on 1 April 2012, after the credit evaluation was completed.
Subsequently, I-Connect defaulted on some payments under the IC2 Contract of $557,921.50, referred to as the IC Debt.
In June 2014, Wong approved the write-off of the IC Debt.
On 5 March 2014, FXS entered into the MOS1 Contract with MOS to supply offshore diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for $1 million.
On 1 December 2014, FXS entered into the MOS2 Contract with MOS to sell diving equipment and repair, refurbish and test a transformer and capacitor load bank for $2 million.
On 28 August 2015, FXS entered into the MOS3 Contract with MOS to lease diving equipment and provide testing, commissioning and installation services for $1 million.
On 4 November 2015, FXS entered into the MOS4 Contract with MOS to lease diving equipment and provide repair and reconditioning work on a transformer and capacitor load bank unit for $1,007,496.
Subsequently, MOS defaulted on payments under the MOS Contracts, amounting to $2,322,464.28.

On 6 December 2013, FXS entered into a sale agreement with Supreme Lion to supply products and services for the installation, testing and commissioning of power control units for woodwork machinery systems.
Supreme Lion had paid FXS pursuant to the SL Contract.

The 2006 Communication Matrix required concurrence from the President of Asia Pacific Operations for certain business activities outside the territory and for write-offs of bad debts above specified thresholds.
On 2 December 2013, an email attached and informed about the 2013 Communication Matrix to various persons including Wong.
FXS' 2015 Operating Procedure Manual was silent on the requirement for PGs.
The CE Form for the IC1 Contract was submitted on 19 October 2011.
The CE Form for the IC2 Contract was submitted on 29 March 2012.
Three PGs were obtained for each IC Contract, two of whom were directors and shareholders of I-Connect including Raden.
For the MOS2 Contract, FXS obtained personal guarantees from Samuel Yeo, Rachael Yeo and Elizabeth Lee.
For the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, FXS obtained personal guarantees from Samuel Yeo, Rachael Yeo, Elizabeth Lee and Adrian Lim.
The CE Form showed that Samuel's and Rachael's combined annual income at the material time was about $675,000.
The CE Form for the MOS4 Contract showed Samuel's and Rachael's combined annual income based on the income tax assessments had increased to about $1.46 million.
The search on Suleman was made in August 2013 and showed the bankruptcy application against Suleman was filed only in July 2013.
The result of the search on Raden would seem to have been made known to Wong only in June 2014, after FXS had chased for the IC Debt and Wong was approving its write-off.
A purchase order dated 19 December 2014 issued by FXS to Serve Tech pertaining to the MOS2 Contract stated that the purchase order was subject to the terms and conditions appearing overleaf.
The purchase order between FXS and HM contained a warranty and indemnity which bound HM and protected FXS.

The Abacus System had limitations in that the description for a particular transaction had to be chosen from a drop-down box and there was no description corresponding to an instalment sale agreement.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company is legally justified in summarily dismissing its CEO and withholding bonuses based on internal audit findings of irregular transactions.
Party_Details.Rule: An employer seeking to rely on contractual summary termination provisions must the employee's conduct falls within the defined categories such as causing material damage, gross default or misconduct, or conduct grossly incompatible with the due and faithful discharge of duties. Senior managers and directors are act with reasonable care, skill and diligence, to comply with internal approval matrices and credit procedures, and to manage transactional risk through appropriate documentation and oversight. Where summary termination is not justified, damages for dismissal are assessed under the minimum legal obligation principle, limiting the employer's liability to the least costly lawful method of terminating the contract, typically by exercising a contractual right to terminate on notice or with salary in lieu, and taking into account any contractual entitlements that would have accrued during that period.
Party_Details.Application: The legal conflict evaluates whether the company's decision to terminate its CEO without notice and withhold his variable bonus was a lawful response to audit findings indicating that he had breached his directorial duties through irregular transactions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's reliance on clauses 10(b), (h) and (i) of Wong's Employment Contract to justify summary dismissal. It examined the company's internal governance framework, including the 2006 and 2013 Communication Matrices and the credit evaluation process, and the factual circumstances of the I-Connect, MOS and Supreme Lion transactions. The court found that the IC1 and IC2 Contracts were project management-type arrangements with back-to-back supplier contracts and that the Tsalasa Contract contained warranties and undertakings protecting Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd. It noted that I-Connect did not allege breach and that suppliers performed their obligations. The court accepted evidence that Wong consulted relevant departments, that the Legal Department cleared the IC1 Contract, and that the then President of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa, orally approved the IC1 and IC2 Contracts at a Business Review Meeting, thereby satisfying or curing any Communication Matrix approval requirement. It held that although Wong did not initially obtain formal approval before entering the IC1 Contract or before writing off the IC Debt, subsequent approval by the regional president and the board, including Honda's acceptance of the audited accounts reflecting the write-off, rectified any procedural breach. The court concluded that these matters did not cause material damage to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and did not amount to conduct grossly incompatible with Wong's duties or to gross default or misconduct under clause 10(b), (h) or (i).

For the MOS transactions, the court rejected Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's characterisation of the MOS2, MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts as pure loans disguised as sales or rentals. It relied on the contractual documents, supplier quotations and purchase orders, manual costing sheets, and testimony from finance staff to find that the MOS2 Contract was an instalment sale with a gross profit margin and that the MOS3 and MOS4 Contracts, though documented as rental agreements due to limitations of the Abacus billing system, were in substance asset sale or lease transactions with underlying goods and services supplied by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd through its suppliers. The court noted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd contracted directly with suppliers, that goods were delivered and no complaints were raised by MOS, and that the Legal Department had cleared the MOS2 Contract. It accepted that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was already providing commercial financing and project management services and that the MOS transactions fell within an expanded business model encouraged by regional management. The court found that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd obtained multiple personal guarantees from individuals associated with MOS, that detailed credit evaluations were performed, and that MOS had a positive payment history before later default. It held that the company failed to show that Wong exposed it to unreasonable or unnecessary liability or that he failed to exercise reasonable diligence.

Regarding the Supreme Lion transaction, the court found that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd entered into a sale agreement to supply woodwork machinery system products and services, that it contracted with Hyper Marketing (Singapore) Pte Ltd under purchase orders incorporating standard terms, warranties and indemnities, and that Supreme Lion paid the contract price without raising complaints. The court rejected the assertion that the supplier lacked capability based solely on an ACRA search conducted years later and held that there was no evidence of loss or improper exposure arising from this transaction.

The court evaluated the Communication Matrices and concluded that while certain approvals were formally required for business activities outside the territory, new business activities, and write-offs above specified thresholds, oral approvals by the Presidents of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa and later Honda, satisfied or cured these requirements. It accepted that the matrices did not convert every procedural misstep into a repudiatory breach and noted that clause 10(c) of the Employment Contract contemplated warnings and repetition for violations of work regulations. Applying the standard of reasonable care and diligence from Scintronix and the principles on serious misconduct and repudiatory breach from Phosagro, the court held that Wong's conduct did not reach the threshold of gross default or misconduct or conduct grossly incompatible with his duties, and that any technical breaches of internal procedures were either rectified or non-prejudicial.

On Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd's counterclaim for approximately SGD 2.88 million, the court held that the company had not established that Wong's conduct caused the IC Debt or the MOS outstanding amounts in a way that would ground liability for breach of fiduciary or contractual duties. It therefore dismissed the counterclaim. On the lawfulness of the summary dismissal, the court concluded that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was not entitled to rely on clause 10(b), (h) or (i) to summarily dismiss Wong without notice or payment in lieu. It then applied the minimum legal obligation rule from Alexander Proudfoot and Gunton, as elaborated in Aldabe Fermin, to determine damages. The court reasoned that, given the audit findings and the employer's subsequent actions, Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd would have chosen to terminate Wong's employment on 2017-12-21 by exercising its contractual right under clause 8.1 to pay three months' basic salary in lieu of notice rather than allowing the contract to run to 2018-03-31 or giving working notice. It therefore assessed Wong's entitlement on the basis of a lawful termination with three months' salary in lieu of notice and considered his claims for notice pay, end-of-term payment, variable bonus and salary in lieu of accrued leave accordingly. The court held that the summary dismissal was unlawful and that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was liable for wrongful dismissal within the limits of the minimum legal obligation framework.

Metadata.Coram: Wong Sung Boon v Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and another
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: From around 2016-07-01, Katsumi Kizaki served as General Manager of the Legal Department of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd.
At material times, the President of Asia Pacific Operations also held the position of President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd and was a director of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd.

From 2017-04 to 2017-06, an Independent Investigation Committee established by Fujifilm conducted an investigation into accounting practices in Fuji Xerox Australia and Fuji Xerox New Zealand, subsidiaries of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd.

Around 2017-09-01, Fujifilm's Global Audit Division commenced investigations into allegations of serious misconduct relating to Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd, in parallel with an audit investigation by Deloitte & Touche Financial Advisory Services Pte Ltd.

In early 2012, during a Business Review Meeting, the then President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, Katsuhiko Yanagawa, was informed by Wong that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd had entered into the IC1 Contract and intended to enter into the IC2 Contract with I-Connect Interactive Pte Ltd.

In early 2014, during a Business Review Meeting, the then President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, Masashi Honda, was informed by Wong that Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd was considering transactions with M.O.S. Marine Offshore Services Pte Ltd involving diving equipment.

In 2015, as President of Asia Pacific Operations and President of Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, Masashi Honda attended the annual general meeting of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and signed off on its audited accounts for the financial year April 2014 to March 2015, which reflected the write-off of the IC Debt.

On 2013-12-02, Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd circulated the 2013 Communication Matrix by email to senior personnel in group companies, including Wong, setting out approval requirements for new business activities, unique products and write-offs of debts above specified amounts.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a regional parent company is liable for inducing a breach of contract and conspiracy when its audit directives result in a subsidiary terminating its CEO.
Party_Details.Rule: A parent or regional holding company may be in conspiracy or for inducing of contract if it intentionally participates in a combination to cause unlawful injury or knowingly and intentionally procures a subsidiary's of contract without justification. Group-level oversight, audit activities and approval of subsidiary accounts, when carried out within the scope of corporate governance, do not by themselves constitute inducement of . The existence of internal communication matrices and audit reports may inform whether the parent had knowledge of relevant facts, but liability requires proof of intentional procurement of a contractual rather than mere exercise of oversight.
Party_Details.Application: A regional parent company oversaw audits that led to a subsidiary terminating its CEO. The dispute concerns whether the parent company's oversight and directives constituted legitimate corporate governance or an actionable tort of inducing a breach of the CEO's employment contract.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court considered Wong's claims that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd conspired with Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to injure him by dismissal or wrongfully induced Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to breach his Employment Contract. It examined the corporate structure, noting that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd owned Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and that the Presidents of Asia Pacific Operations, Yanagawa and later Honda, held positions in both companies and sat on the board of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd. The court reviewed the evidence of Business Review Meetings at which Wong presented the I-Connect and MOS transactions to the regional presidents, and the circulation of the 2013 Communication Matrix by Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd to senior personnel, including Wong. It also noted that Honda, as regional president and director, attended the annual general meeting of Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and signed off on audited accounts reflecting the write-off of the IC Debt.

The court accepted that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, through its presidents and legal department, exercised oversight and governance over Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd, including setting approval frameworks and participating in group-level investigations and audits. However, it found no evidence that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd, as a separate legal entity, had directed or procured Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd to terminate Wong's Employment Contract in breach of its terms. The termination notice was issued by Fuji Xerox Singapore Pte Ltd and relied on clauses in Wong's Employment Contract. The court held that group-level audit findings and oversight, including the Deloitte Report and the Fujifilm Global Audit Division special audit report, did not by themselves amount to inducement of breach or conspiracy. It concluded that Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd's actions were consistent with its role in corporate governance and did not satisfy the elements of conspiracy or inducement of breach of contract. Accordingly, the claims against Fuji Xerox Asia Pacific Pte Ltd were dismissed and it was not held liable for Wong's unlawful dismissal or for any of the alleged transactional losses.
